terms.definition: The official document or device used to cast a vote in an election.
A meeting of party members to select candidates or decide policy, used in some states as an alternative to primaries.
A geographic area whose residents are represented by an elected official.
A person authorized to represent others at a political convention or assembly.
A body of electors established by the U.S. Constitution to elect the president and vice president.
The right to vote in public elections, also known as suffrage.
The practice of drawing electoral district boundaries to favor a particular party or group.
The current holder of a political office who is running for re-election.
A ballot that is mailed to voters and returned by mail, allowing voting without visiting a polling station.
An election held in the middle of a president's four-year term, for congressional and state offices.
A designated location where voters go to cast their ballots on election day.
An election in which party members vote to select their party's candidate for the general election.
An electoral system where seats are allocated based on the proportion of votes each party receives.
A voting method where voters rank candidates in order of preference; if no candidate wins a majority, the lowest-ranked candidate is eliminated and votes redistributed.
The process of redrawing electoral district boundaries, typically after a census.
A direct vote by the electorate on a specific proposal or issue, rather than on a candidate.
A second election held when no candidate achieves the required majority in the first round.
The right to vote in political elections.
A state where both major political parties have similar levels of support and the outcome is uncertain.
The process by which eligible citizens sign up to be added to the official list of voters.
The percentage of eligible voters who actually cast a ballot in an election.
A method that allows voters to cast their ballot before election day, typically by mail, when they cannot visit the polling station.
Involving cooperation or agreement between two major political parties.
The systematic process of directly contacting voters to gather support or information, or the official tabulation of votes after an election.
The revocation or denial of the right to vote for an individual or group.
